title: DOM Manipulation
summary: The DOM is the browser's live model of the page.
JavaScript can read and change it.
You select an element, then change its text, style, or content.
This is how pages become interactive.

sections.text: When a page loads, the browser builds a living model of it that JavaScript can touch. Change that model, and the page on screen changes too. This is how a static page becomes interactive.

sections.title: What the DOM Is
sections.text: The DOM, short for Document Object Model, is the browser's representation of your page as a tree of objects, one for each element. It is live. If your code changes the DOM, the visible page updates to match.

sections.title: Selecting and Changing
sections.text: To change something, you first select the element you want, usually by a class or id. Then you can change its text, swap its content, or adjust its style. Select, then change. That two step pattern is the heart of DOM work.

media.alt: An element being selected, then its text changing

sections.title: A Remote Control for the Page
sections.text: The DOM is like a remote control for the page. Each element is a device you can point at. First you choose which device, that is selecting. Then you press a button to change it, louder, brighter, off. The screen responds right away.

media.alt: A remote pointing at an element and changing it

sections.title: Changing the Page
items: Wait for the page to load.
Select the element you want to change.
Read or change its text or content.
Adjust its style if you need to.
The visible page updates to match.

sections.text: Changing the DOM many times in a row can slow a page down, because each change can make the browser redo work. This is why frameworks like React were created, to do it efficiently.

terms.definition: The browser's live model of the page.
One item on the page, like a button or heading.
Choosing an element to work with.
The nested structure of all the page's elements.
A change to the DOM that the page reflects.

options: The browser's live model of the page
questions.explanation: The DOM is the browser's live model of the page as a tree of elements.

questions.text: What two steps change the page?
options: Select an element, then change it
questions.explanation: You select the element, then change it. That two step pattern is the heart of DOM work.

questions.text: What happens when JavaScript changes the DOM?
options: The visible page updates to match
The page reloads from the server
questions.explanation: The DOM is live, so changing it updates the visible page to match.
